svg.title: Swapping in Operating Systems
svg.footer: Learn about swapping in operating systems with this animated diagram.

text.style: font-size: 18px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; opacity: 0.8
font-size: 16px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; opacity: 0.8
text.data-narration: Swapping is a memory management technique used by operating systems to manage memory allocation and deallocation efficiently. It involves temporarily storing frequently used data in a faster, more efficient storage medium, such as disk storage, while the main memory is used for other tasks.

g.data-narration: Page Replacement: When the main memory is full and a new page needs to be added, the operating system selects a page to be swapped out.

text.style: font-size: 14px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; opacity: 0.8
text.data-narration: The page that is swapped out is stored on disk storage. A clock or FIFO queue is used to determine which page to swap out next.

g.data-narration: Page Fault: When a process accesses a page that is currently stored on disk storage, a page fault occurs. The operating system then reads the page from disk storage into main memory.
Swapping Out: The page that was swapped out is removed from main memory and stored on disk storage.
There are two types of swapping:

text.style: font-size: 14px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; opacity: 0.8
text.data-narration: Clock Swapping: This method uses a clock to determine which page to swap out. When all pages are in memory, the clock is reset and the process starts over.

text.style: font-size: 14px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; opacity: 0.8
text.data-narration: FIFO Swapping: This method uses a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue to determine which page to swap out. The page that was added to memory first is the first to be swapped out.

g.data-narration: Advantages include memory efficiency and improved system performance. However, disadvantages include slow page replacement and increased disk I/O.

For example, if a process is running and needs to access a page that is not in main memory, a page fault occurs. The operating system reads the page from disk storage into main memory, a process known as swapping out.

footer.a: Learn more about swapping in operating systems
